explanation_md: `inner()` throws `std::logic_error`. Stack unwinding passes through `middle()` (no local handler). The catch in `main` accepts `const std::exception&` — a base class reference — which matches `std::logic_error` (which derives from `std::logic_error` → `std::exception`). The handler prints `e.what()` which returns "oops".

prompt_md: In a catch handler, you write `throw;` (with no operand). What does this do compared to `throw e;`?

options.a: Both are identical — they rethrow the same exception object.
options.b: `throw;` rethrows the original exception object without slicing; `throw e;` may create a new copy of the caught type, potentially slicing if `e` is a base reference to a derived exception.
options.c: `throw;` terminates the program; `throw e;` propagates the exception.
options.d: `throw;` only works inside function try blocks; `throw e;` works anywhere.

explanation_md: `throw;` re-activates the currently active exception — the original derived object — without any copying or slicing. `throw e;` when `e` is a `const std::exception&` constructs a new `std::exception` from the reference, discarding any derived-class data. Always use `throw;` to rethrow from a catch handler when you want to preserve the original exception.

prompt_md: Which ordering of catch clauses is correct when you want to handle `std::runtime_error` specifically and all other `std::exception` types generically?

options.a: `catch (const std::exception& e)` first, then `catch (const std::runtime_error& e)`.
options.b: `catch (const std::runtime_error& e)` first, then `catch (const std::exception& e)`.
options.c: Order does not matter — the most-derived handler always wins.
options.d: You cannot catch both in the same try block.

explanation_md: Catch clauses are tried in order. If `std::exception` appears first, it matches every standard exception including `std::runtime_error`, leaving the second handler unreachable. The most-derived (most-specific) type must come first so it gets a chance to match before the more general handler swallows everything.

options.a: save() should be called in the constructor, not the destructor.
options.b: The destructor throws an exception, which calls std::terminate if the destructor runs during stack unwinding.
options.c: The if condition is inverted — it should throw when save() returns true.

explanation_md: Destructors are implicitly `noexcept`. If `~Widget()` runs while another exception is propagating (stack unwinding), throwing a second exception causes `std::terminate`. The fix is to wrap `save()` in a try/catch inside the destructor and swallow or log any failure — never allow an exception to escape a destructor.

`noexcept(f())` is a compile-time operator that evaluates to `true` when the expression is guaranteed not to throw. `f()` is declared `noexcept`, so it returns `true`. With `std::boolalpha`, the bool value prints as `true` rather than `1`.
